metadata.summary: Ask users to organize content topics into groups and label those groups, revealing how users mentally categorize information. Use open sort to discover natural categories, closed sort to validate a proposed structure, or hybrid to refine an existing IA. Requires 15-20 participants for open sort, 30+ for statistically meaningful closed sort results.

content.title: Card Sorting
content.summary: Ask users to organize content topics into groups and label those groups, revealing how users mentally categorize information. Use open sort to discover natural categories, closed sort to validate a proposed structure, or hybrid to refine an existing IA. Requires 15-20 participants for open sort, 30+ for statistically meaningful closed sort results.

why.p: Navigation structures designed by product teams mirror internal org charts, not user mental models. Card sorting surfaces the vocabulary and groupings real users expect, before the navigation is built. Co-occurrence matrices identify strong clusters (greater than 70% agreement) and problem items that need further investigation.

list.item: You do not know how users think about your content or what labels they expect
Users cannot find content in the current navigation (high search usage, low direct nav success)
Designing or redesigning a site/app information architecture from scratch or major overhaul
Analyzing open-sort exports to compute similarity matrices and candidate IA structure
Fewer than 10 content items — overhead not justified; use interviews or usability tests instead
IA already validated through tree testing and live search analytics — re-sorting adds noise
UI layout or visual hierarchy questions — card sorting answers content organization, not placement
As a substitute for running the actual study — agents cannot observe real-time hesitation or card-splitting

section.name: card-sorting-process
section.p: Six-step process for planning and running card sort studies to inform information architecture decisions.

rule.statement: State specific research questions before selecting card sort type or preparing cards.
rule.rationale: Goals determine whether to run open (discover categories), closed (validate structure), or hybrid (refine existing IA) sort. Mixing goals in one study produces inconclusive data.

example.note: Research questions: How do users group our content? What labels make sense to users? Does our navigation match user mental models?

rule.statement: Use 30-60 cards with real content labels written in user language, not internal terminology.
rule.rationale: Over 60 cards causes cognitive fatigue. Jargon on cards tests familiarity with internal terminology rather than mental models. Under 10 items does not justify the study overhead.

rule.statement: Recruit 15-20 participants for open sort; 30+ for closed sort statistical validity.
rule.rationale: Below 15 participants, co-occurrence clusters are statistically unstable. Closed sort requires more participants because placement percentages need sufficient N to be meaningful.

rule.statement: Give participants clear, neutral instructions that do not prime any grouping scheme.
rule.rationale: Priming instructions contaminate results. Instructions must state the task without suggesting categories or grouping logic.

example.note: Open sort: "Please organize these cards into groups that make sense to you. You can create as many groups as you need. When done, give each group a name."

rule.statement: Compute co-occurrence rates for all card pairs; apply thresholds: above 70% — group together; 40-70% — borderline; below 40% — keep separate.
rule.rationale: Co-occurrence data reveals which groupings are statistically robust vs. which reflect individual preferences. Outlier cards (below 40% with any partner) indicate content needing redesign, not just recategorization.

section.name: analysis-methods
section.p: Co-occurrence matrix construction, similarity thresholds, standardization metric, and outlier interpretation rules.

rule.statement: For each pair of cards, compute the percentage of participants who grouped them together; this is the co-occurrence rate.
rule.rationale: Co-occurrence data reveals statistically robust groupings independent of the category labels participants used, which vary widely between individuals in open sorts.

rule.statement: Apply three-tier thresholds: above 70% co-occurrence — group together in IA; 40-70% — consider grouping, investigate further; below 40% — keep separate or investigate content problem.
rule.rationale: These thresholds are empirically calibrated for 15-20 participant studies. Below 15 participants, all thresholds are statistically meaningless — report sample size prominently when under threshold.

rule.statement: Cards with no co-occurrence above 40% with any partner are outliers — these indicate content that needs redesign (renaming, splitting, or merging), not just recategorization.
rule.rationale: Outlier cards signal that users cannot form a mental model for the content itself, not that the categorization is wrong. Forcing outlier cards into categories produces navigation that feels arbitrary.

rule.statement: Measure standardization (how consistently participants created similar categories) as an overall study quality indicator.
rule.rationale: Low standardization across participants may indicate cards are ambiguous, participants are too heterogeneous, or the content domain has no shared mental model — all of which affect how much weight to give the results.

list.item: High standardization: most users created similar categories — results are reliable, proceed to tree testing
Low standardization: each user had a unique approach — investigate card label clarity before building IA from this data

rule.statement: Always run tree testing on the IA structure proposed from card sort results before building; card sorting shows grouping preferences, not findability.
rule.rationale: Card sorting tells you how users group content; it does not tell you whether users can find content in a hierarchical navigation built from those groupings. Tree testing is the validation step.

section.name: examples-and-agentic-workflow
section.p: Open sort and closed sort examples with real data, common mistakes, and agentic co-occurrence analysis workflow.

example.note: E-commerce product categories open sort, 20 participants. Results: 15/20 grouped "Laptops" and "Desktops" together. Participants named this group "Computers" (not "Computing Devices" which was the internal term). "Tablets" split: 8 users with computers, 7 with phones. Insight: Create "Computers" category using user vocabulary; run tree test with Tablets in both locations to determine final placement.

Categories: Electronics, Home, Clothing. Smart Watch: 85% Electronics, 5% Home, 10% Clothing — clear placement. Heated Blanket: 40% Electronics, 55% Home, 5% Clothing — ambiguous, needs further investigation. Fitness Tracker: 70% Electronics, 10% Home, 20% Clothing — acceptable placement with minor ambiguity toward Clothing.

antipattern.description: Using 80+ cards in a single session.
antipattern.reason: Cognitive fatigue sets in above 60 cards. Participants start grouping quickly without careful consideration, producing lower-quality data. Split into two separate focused studies.
Jargon tests vocabulary familiarity, not mental models. Cards must use user language. If your sitemap says "Resource Hub", the card must say "Guides and Templates".

antipattern.description: Running closed sort without running open sort first.
antipattern.reason: Closed sort can only validate categories you already have. Open sort discovers the categories users actually expect. Running closed sort first bakes in untested assumptions.

section.p: Agent receives content inventory (list of page/item names) and generates a card list with jargon replaced by user-friendly language. After study runs, agent receives exported participant groupings as JSON/CSV, computes co-occurrence matrix, identifies strong clusters (above 70%), borderline items (40-70%), and outliers (below 40%). Proposes candidate IA structure. Generates tree test spec as mandatory follow-up.

antipattern.description: Agent produces confident IA recommendations from fewer than 15 participants.
antipattern.reason: Below 15 participants, co-occurrence clusters are statistically unstable. Flag sample size prominently in output and label recommendations as hypotheses requiring validation.

antipattern.description: Agent treats card sort results as final without mandating tree testing.
antipattern.reason: Card sorting shows how users group content; it does not validate that users can find content in a hierarchy built from those groupings. Tree testing is always required before building.

template.purpose: Study plan: objectives, method, participant profile, card list, categories (closed), analysis plan
Results template: category structure, agreement matrix, key insights, IA recommendations
Python script: compute co-occurrence matrix from open sort export; identify strong/weak/outlier clusters
